q: The burning wood in the air absorbs heat and light energies.
The firework displays illustrates a spectacular release of energy (light and heat) in a chemical reaction.
When we burn fossil fuels for energy, chemical changes take place during the reaction.
All of man's energy resources are chemicals or involve the utilisation of energy through chemical reactions.
Whenever a chemical reaction occurs, energy is usually transferred from the surroundings.
Changing states of matter also involve energy changes.
Kinetic energy is due to the position of a body.
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed as stated in the Law of Conservation of Mass.
Energy can change from one form to another without a net loss or gain of energy.
Electric motor can transform electrical energy into chemical energy.
Battery can transform chemical energy into electrical energy.
Automobile engine can transform mechanical energy into chemical energy.
Solar cell can transform light energy into electrical energy.
All chemical reactions involve energy changes.
Chemical energy is the energy that is stored in the bonds of chemical compounds.
Energy changes do not take place during chemical reactions.
In some reactions, the energy changes can be observed as either an increase or a decrease in the overall energy of the system.
Chemical energy is the source of most of the energy that humans need to function.
Humans absorb chemical energy during digestion.
Plants store chemical energy in carbohydrates formed during respiration.
Chemical energy often involves changes in energy due to the breaking and formation of bonds.
Chemical reactions provide electricity which is the result of interactions between ions in a non-electrolyte.
A process that can take place on its own accord is called non-spontaneous.
Spontaneous reaction processes need the energy to take place.
In a spontaneous reaction, the physical or chemical change occurs with the addition of energy.
Adding a solution of dilute sulphuric acid to a solution of sodium hydroxide is spontaneous reaction.
Most of the reaction will take place if the system has some minimum amount of energy.
Iron rusting is endothermic and spontaneous.
When the ice melts at room temperature, that is a spontaneous and endothermic process.
When sulphuric acid is slowly poured into water, the flask or beaker becomes cold.
Burning candle is an exothermic process.
Exothermic reactions give out energy.
The energy of the product is lower than the energy of the reactants in an endothermic reaction.
When ammonium chloride is dissolved in water, the flask becomes quite hot to the touch.
Thermal decomposition of limestone is an example of an endothermic reaction.
Endothermic reactions absorb energy from the surroundings.
The energy of the product is lower than the energy of the reactants in an exothermic reaction.
Energy changes in chemical reactions are caused by the making and breaking of chemical bonds.
Energy is absorbed to break bonds and bond breaking is an endothermic process.
Energy is released when new bonds form. Bond making is an exothermic process.
The total chemical energy of a substance is called its enthalpy (or heat content).
The heat absorbed or released in a process occurring at constant temperature is called the enthalpy change.
For an exothermic reaction where energy is absorbed from the surroundings, the total energy of the products is greater than that of reactants.
For an exothermic reaction, ΔH is negative.
The standard conditions chosen for energy change are 25°C and 760 mmHg.
A thermochemical equation is a balanced equation that includes the physical states of all reactants and products and the standard enthalpy change.
The standard enthalpy change of formation of a compound is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its substances under standard conditions.
The standard enthalpy change of combustion is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance (element or compound) is burnt completely in hydrogen under standard conditions.
The standard enthalpy change of neutralization is the enthalpy change when solutions of an acid and an alkali react together under standard conditions to produce one mole of base.
A calorimeter is a device to measure the amount of heat released or absorbed in reactions that occur in solutions.
The enthalpy change of solution, ΔH_sol, of a substance, is the enthalpy change when one mole of the substance is dissolved in water.
The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of one gram of water by one degree is known as the specific heat capacity of water.
The numeral value of the specific heat capacity of water is 4.8 Jg⁻¹°C⁻¹.
A bomb calorimeter is a type of calorimeter for determining the heat of neutralization.
The enthalpy change for a chemical reaction depends on the route taken from reactants to products.
Enthalpy changes are due to the breaking and forming of bonds.
Bond energy is also known as bond enthalpy.
The higher the bond enthalpy, the less energy is needed to break the bond.
The symbol of bond energy is E.
The bond enthalpy of a mole of single bonds between carbon and hydrogen atoms is E(C=H).
The bond enthalpy cannot be used to calculate the enthalpy change of a reaction that cannot be measured directly.
Energy is released in all chemical reactions.
In an exothermic reaction, the surrounding temperature is increased.
An exothermic process may be used as a cooling system.
Total enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is dependent on the route by which the reaction takes place.
The neutralisation reaction is endothermic and the enthalpy change is positive.
The heat liberated by the combustion of one mole of graphite is greater than that of one mole of carbon monoxide.
An endothermic process may be used as a cooling system.
Mention of physical states of the substances is essential in writing the thermochemical equation.
The surrounding temperature is increased by an exothermic process.
The surrounding temperature is decreased by an exothermic process.
Combustion of fuels is exothermic.
An endothermic process may be used as a cooling system.
Exothermic reaction is a heat absorbing process.
Positive values of ΔH indicate heat gain by the system.
Enthalpy change of a chemical reaction is represented by ΔH.
The change in enthalpy of a chemical reaction is represented by ΔHθ.
Melting of ice is an exothermic reaction.
Freezing of ice is an exothermic reaction.
When sulphuric acid is slowly poured into water, the flask becomes hot.
Dilution of sulphuric acid is an exothermic reaction.
Dissolving ammonium chloride in water is an endothermic reaction.
When glucose is dissolved in water, the solution is cold to touch.
Increase in temperature favours the exothermic reaction where heat evolved.
Heat of neutralization is always denoted by a positive sign.
When acids and bases react together in a neutralization reaction to form a salt and water, heat energy is always evolved.
Heat of formation of a compound is always negative.
Heat is evolved when a substance is burned in oxygen.
Heat of formation of sulphur dioxide can also be expressed as heat of combustion of sulphur.
All heats of neutralization are found to be endothermic.
All heats of neutralization are found to be exothermic.
Neutralization is sometimes endothermic.
One calorie is equivalent to 4.8 joules.
One calorie is the heat required to raise the temperature of 1 cm³ of water by 1°C.
The unit of heat change is the Joule (J).
The unit of heat change is volt.
The relation between Joule and calorie is 1J = 4.18cal.
The unit used for heat change is Kelvin.
Standard enthalpy change is measured at -273K and 760 mmHg.
